S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Use Claude to generate lesson-plan drafts anchored in clear learning outcomes.
Audit a Claude-generated plan for cognitive labor whether students are doing the thinking.
· Redesign teacher-centered sequences into active learning alternatives.
· Use Claude to produce targeted lesson components rather than complete plans.
· Apply a five-question review gate before using any Claude-generated plan.
data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: CORE CONCEPT — BACKWARD DESIGN AND COGNITIVE LABOR
blocks: Two principles govern good lesson planning. The first is backward design. The second is cognitive labor.
Core Concept — Backward Design and Cogni — Backward design, developed by Wiggins and McTighe in Understanding by Design, holds that planning should begin with the

data.title: A WORKED TEACHING WALKTHROUGH.
blocks: · "Draft three discussion questions for this activity. Each question should be unanswerable by recalling a definition."
· "Generate four plausible student thesis statements ranging from vague to specific, using this topic. I will use these as
"Draft an exit ticket with two questions one that assesses whether students achieved the outcome, and one that asks them to name one thing they are still unsure
· "Suggest two formative-check moments I can embed in this sequence without adding more than five minutes total."

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE — WHAT STAYS WITH YOU
blocks: Pacing and classroom reality.
Content accuracy.

data.section: THE HUMAN GATE — WHAT STAYS WI

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Mistake 1: Accepting the complete plan.
Mistake 2: Requesting a lesson plan without stating the outcome first.
Mistake 3: Keeping the teacher at the center of every activity.
Mistake 4: Using Claude's examples without checking them.
Mistake 5: Ignoring the formative feedback loop.
